topic: Eclipse geometry §4.2.7 — EXP-272 patches verifying ISS-1607 (SVG static-spot framing) and ISS-1608 (lateral-velocity / angular-velocity propagation)
our_argument_summary: §4.2.7 argues that the dome's published Sun (5,733 km) and Moon (2,534 km) altitudes plus observed angular sizes force a totality footprint roughly 1.3 km wide and 0.9–3 sec per single observer, vs. NASA's 294-km path and 138-sec maximum totality for the Aug 12, 2026 eclipse. The dome must accept one of three concessions: parameters wrong, prediction parasitic on globe geometry, or eclipse not occultation. EXP-272 fixed two issues raised by the c1 review of EXP-254: (a) the H3 prose parenthetical's claim that 25 km/min orbits 'leave the totality-circle diameter unchanged' was misleading (true of diameter, false of per-observer duration); and (b) the SVG annotation panel still framed the dome footprint as a 'totality circle' / 'stationary spot' and had a redundant red patch line.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: The dome page lists seven station-level magnetic-field eclipse predictions (Ebro, San Pablo, Eskdalemuir, Lerwick, Hartland, Niemegk, Canary Islands) with FSF and BOU/W004 nT predictions. Coverage values per station (0.40–0.95) are taken from NASA Besselian elements — the dome page does not derive station selection from V51 dome geometry. E-PRED-A explicitly states 'Peak tracks eclipse geometry, not solar noon' — i.e., it relies on the standard eclipse path-of-totality calculation. The dome publishes no aetheric/non-occultation eclipse mechanism in formula_runtime.json or model.html.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Coverage values appear hardcoded from NASA path-of-totality data; FSF values are computed via the FSF formula but FSF itself is the tan(latitude) tautology established in §4.2.4. Magnetic predictions are then derived from coverage × FSF × baseline.
data_sources_used: NASA Besselian elements (implicitly, for coverage values)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The dome could in principle publish an aetheric eclipse mechanism that bypasses geometric occultation; §4.2.7 explicitly anticipates this with the parenthetical 'If a non-geometric mechanism is later published, the section that follows changes; until then, occultation is the only mechanism on the table.' Adequate escape clause coverage.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None identified for this expansion. The coverage-comes-from-NASA point is intact and consistent with raw-text/03-predictions.txt.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Section-level review; the underlying eclipse predictions in monitor.py do live-fetch magnetometer data (status_history.json reports BOU/W004 readings) so the eclipse comparison side fetches real observatory data. The geometry argument itself does not need monitoring — it's a closed-form calculation.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: The argument here is geometric, not relabel — we're computing what the dome's parameters predict and comparing against NASA's eclipse path data. No standard-physics phenomenon is being relabelled.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Eclipse occurs Aug 12, 2026 — predictions registered 2026-03-12 per raw-text/03-predictions.txt. Genuinely prospective from the dome's side. Our analysis uses dome's published parameters and NASA's pre-published Besselian elements.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: We DO derive from dome geometry — h_Sun=5,733, h_Moon=2,534, then compute R_u, R_p, totality duration. The derivation chain is traceable end-to-end and uses only dome-published parameters plus observed angular sizes.

holes_found.description: The 'argument is robust either way' claim is true but the prose doesn't explicitly close the defender's 'orbit the moon even slower' escape route. EXP-272's math_verification box notes that even matching the actual moon's observed angular motion (~0.25°/min, giving v ≈ 11 km/min at h=2,534 km) yields totality at one observer of only ~7 sec — still ~20× short of the observed 138 sec. This decisive defensive material lives in the EXP-272 worksheet but does not appear in the integrated prose. A defender confronted with the published §4.2.7 can ask 'why did you stop at 25 km/min?' and the answer requires referencing the angular-motion argument, which the section does not currently make.
holes_found.recommendation: Add one sentence after 'argument is robust either way' along the lines of: 'The slowest velocity consistent with the actual Moon's observed apparent motion (~0.25°/min) corresponds to v ≈ 11 km/min at h_Moon = 2,534 km, which would still produce per-observer totality of only ~7 seconds — a 20× shortfall. Slowing the dome moon further breaks the actual sky observation.' This converts a kernel-of-truth admission into an explicit closure of the slow-orbit escape.

holes_found.description: Residual static-framing language survives in three secondary annotations: (1) SVG `<desc>` element line 3 — 'produces a totality zone roughly 1.3 km wide'; (2) SVG callout label at line 129 — '~1.3 km totality zone'; (3) IMG alt text in part4.html — 'roughly 1.3-km totality zone with 85-km penumbra.' The substantive bullet panel and the H3 prose are now correctly framed as 'track' / 'moves' / per-observer dwell. These three remaining 'zone' instances are arguable — 'zone' can describe an instantaneous geometric region without implying staticness — but a defender looking for rhetorical openings will still find them. ISS-1607 was specifically about 'stationary spot' framing; the substantive fix is in, but it's a partial cleanup.
holes_found.recommendation: Optional follow-up patch: change SVG `<desc>` + line 129 callout + IMG alt text from 'totality zone' to 'totality footprint' (instantaneous region) or 'totality track' (time-integrated). 'Footprint' is technically the most accurate term — it names the geometric region without implying duration. Low priority since the bullet panel and prose carry the substantive fix; queue this as a stutter-fix issue rather than a fresh EXP.

holes_found.description: The SVG annotation panel reports only the rim-orbit upper bound (0.9 sec, 1/150 ratio). The patched H3 prose now also presents the slower mid-disc case (3 sec, 1/45 ratio). The figure-text inconsistency is small but a careful reader will notice the prose admits a longer per-observer dwell that the figure suppresses. Defender's framing: 'Even your own diagram shows you cherry-picked the worst case for the dome.'
holes_found.recommendation: Optional: add a fourth bullet to the annotation panel showing the slower-orbit case, e.g. 'or ~3 sec at mid-disc speed (~25 km/min)'. Or more conservatively, change the y=200 bullet to '0.9 sec at one observer (rim orbit; ~3 sec at mid-disc)'. Either keeps the headline 1/150 figure visible while acknowledging the slower case the prose now embraces.

holes_found.description: The new parenthetical's angular-velocity footnote is asymmetrically qualified: '~2°/min apparent shadow speed at zenith' includes the at-zenith caveat, but '~0.5°/min at h_Moon = 2,534 km' does not. Both are zenith values (since ω = v/h holds only when the moon is directly overhead). A picky defender could argue the asymmetry suggests we are sloppy about caveats.
holes_found.recommendation: Tighten to '~0.5°/min at zenith' for symmetry with the rim-orbit phrase. One-character delta in patch terms.

stronger_arguments.description: The angular-motion argument is a separate falsification entirely independent of shadow geometry. The dome moon at 25 km/min produces an apparent angular speed (at zenith) of ~0.57°/min — about 2.3× the observed apparent moon motion of ~0.25°/min (dominated by Earth's rotation in the standard model; in the dome model it's the moon's own orbital motion since the disc is non-rotating). At rim-orbit speed (87 km/min) the dome moon would streak across the sky at ~2°/min — 8× too fast. This is a stand-alone falsification: regardless of shadow width, the dome moon moves at the wrong angular rate. The §4.2.7 prose mentions the observed angular motion implicitly (as the basis for unit-conversion footnotes) but does not turn it into a separate test. Doing so would close the slow-orbit defender escape AND add a second, independent failure mode.
sources: Standard astronomy: lunar synodic motion ≈ 13.2°/day = 0.0092°/min; combined with Earth's diurnal rotation 360°/24h = 15°/h = 0.25°/min, observed apparent moon motion is ≈ 0.24°/min eastward to westward across sky.
EXP-272 math_verification angular_velocity field — already computed 1.97°/min and 0.57°/min for dome model.

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome's lateral shadow ground speed at the mid-disc orbit (25 km/min) is in the same ballpark as NASA's published eclipse-shadow ground speed for the August 2026 path (NASA reports ~30–50 km/min over the central segment). This means the dome author can defensibly point to one numerical alignment with observation — the velocity scale itself is not patently absurd. EXP-272 explicitly acknowledges this kernel.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Velocity scale alignment does not rescue the totality-footprint width or per-observer duration. The dome moon's geometry forces a 1.3-km-wide track regardless of velocity; matching the velocity scale just means the track moves at the right speed across the wrong-shaped path. The prose's 45× duration shortfall claim survives at the slowest plausible orbit. The kernel is acknowledged in the section's framing without rescuing the failure mode.

deeper_analysis: EXP-272 is a clean follow-up to EXP-254. The c1 review of EXP-254 raised three holes (H1, H2, H3); H1 was already addressed in the integrated EXP-254 prose, and EXP-272 closes H2 and H3 specifically. Both patches verify as integrated: (a) the H3 prose parenthetical now correctly propagates 25 km/min into a 3-sec totality figure with the angular-velocity translation, and the 'argument is robust either way' framing replaces the broken 'leaves diameter unchanged' claim; (b) the SVG bullet panel now reads 'totality track' instead of 'totality circle', the redundant red line is replaced with 'Track moves; per-observer ~0.9 sec', and the explicit 294-km observed reference appears in the ratio bullet. Math reproduction passes — 87.33 km/min rim, 25.23 km/min at flat-earth-Mercator 64°N, 19.41 km/min at 70°N, 0.9/3.12/4.02 sec per-observer totality at those velocities, 1.97°/min and 0.566°/min angular speeds at zenith. Robustness factor 138.2/3.12 = 44.3× rounds to the patch's stated ~45×. The four holes I flag above are tightening rather than load-bearing — none undermine the trilemma. The most useful one to raise as a follow-up issue is the angular-motion argument from stronger_arguments above: it converts the kernel-of-truth velocity-scale concession into a stand-alone second falsification, which would significantly harden the section against the 'slow the moon' escape.

citations_verified: EXP-272 math_verification.rim_orbit_velocity (87.33 km/min) — reproduced 87.33 km/min independently
EXP-272 math_verification.mid_disc_velocity (25 km/min @ 64°N flat-earth-Mercator) — reproduced 25.23 km/min

citations_unchecked: NASA Besselian elements for Aug 12, 2026 (Sun semi-diameter 15′47.0″, Moon semi-diameter 16′16.9″, magnitude 1.0386, path 294 km, max duration 138.2 sec) — these belong to the underlying §4.2.7 prose, not the EXP-272 patches; spot-checked against published Five Millennium Catalog values and consistent.
